concept: a tree node holds a value and two child links
cue: Read the constructor: the first argument is the value, the next two are the left and right children.
note: A binary tree is just nodes linked by `left` and `right`; you reach any node by chaining those fields from the root.

cue: The node was built with only a value, so no children were passed in.
note: A missing child is `null`. Every tree function starts by handling that empty case, which is what stops the recursion.

concept: depth is one plus the deeper subtree
cue: Work out each child's depth first, then add one for the current node.
note: The longest path here is 1 to 2 to 4, three nodes deep; the shorter right side does not change the answer.

cue: Return 0 for an empty tree, else one plus the deeper of the two child depths.
prompt: Write `maxDepth(root)` that returns the number of nodes on the longest path from the root down to a leaf. An empty tree has depth 0. Use recursion.

cases.setup: const root = new TreeNode(3, new TreeNode(9), new TreeNode(20, new TreeNode(15), new TreeNode(7)));
const root = new TreeNode(1, new TreeNode(2, new TreeNode(3, new TreeNode(4), null), null), null);

note: Each node is visited once, so this is O(n) time; the call stack goes as deep as the tree, O(h) space where h is the height.
mapsTo: LC 104 · Maximum Depth of Binary Tree

cue: Follow the order: everything in the left subtree, then this node, then the right subtree.
note: On a binary search tree, an inorder walk hands back the values already sorted, since every left value is smaller and every right value is larger.

cue: Recurse left, then push this node's value, then recurse right.
prompt: Write `inorder(root)` that returns an array of the node values in inorder order: left subtree, then the node, then the right subtree. An empty tree gives `[]`.

cases.setup: const root = new TreeNode(1, null, new TreeNode(2, new TreeNode(3), null));
const root = new TreeNode(2, new TreeNode(1), new TreeNode(3));

note: One visit per node makes this O(n) time and O(h) space for the recursion stack.

cue: Push this node's value first, then recurse left, then recurse right.
prompt: Write `preorder(root)` that returns an array of the node values in preorder order: the node first, then its left subtree, then its right subtree. An empty tree gives `[]`.

cases.setup: const root = new TreeNode(1, null, new TreeNode(2, new TreeNode(3), null));
const root = new TreeNode(1, new TreeNode(2), new TreeNode(3));

note: The only change from inorder is where `root.val` goes; preorder puts it first, so the root always leads the array. O(n) time, O(h) space.

concept: inverting swaps left and right at every node
cue: After the swap, the node that was on the left ends up on the right.
note: Inverting mirrors the tree: at each node the two children trade places, all the way down.

cue: Recurse into both children, then swap them at this node and return the node.
prompt: Write `invert(root)` that mirrors the tree in place, swapping the left and right child of every node, and returns the root. For an empty tree return `null`.

cases.setup: const root = new TreeNode(4, new TreeNode(2, new TreeNode(1), new TreeNode(3)), new TreeNode(7, new TreeNode(6), new TreeNode(9))); const inv = invert(root);
cases.call: [inv.val, inv.left.val, inv.right.val, inv.left.left.val, inv.left.right.val, inv.right.left.val, inv.right.right.val]

cases.setup: const root = new TreeNode(1); const inv = invert(root);
const root = new TreeNode(1, new TreeNode(2), new TreeNode(3)); const inv = invert(root);

note: Every node is touched once, so O(n) time; the recursion goes O(h) deep.

concept: compare two trees node by node
cue: Both empty is a match; one empty is not; otherwise compare values and recurse on both sides.
prompt: Write `isSame(p, q)` that returns `true` if the two trees have the same shape and the same values at every position, else `false`.

cases.setup: const p = new TreeNode(1, new TreeNode(2), new TreeNode(3)); const q = new TreeNode(1, new TreeNode(2), new TreeNode(3));
const p = new TreeNode(1, new TreeNode(2), null); const q = new TreeNode(1, null, new TreeNode(2));
const p = new TreeNode(1, new TreeNode(2), null); const q = new TreeNode(1, new TreeNode(9), null);

note: The two null checks are the key: matching values still fail if the shapes differ. O(n) time over the smaller tree, O(h) space.

concept: level order groups nodes by depth
cue: Read the tree row by row from the top; each row becomes one inner array.
note: Breadth-first order walks the tree top to bottom, left to right, so nodes at the same depth land in the same inner array.

cue: Process one level at a time: read all current nodes, collecting their children as the next level.
prompt: Write `levelOrder(root)` that returns an array of arrays, one per level from top to bottom, each holding that level's node values left to right. An empty tree gives `[]`. Walk the tree breadth-first, level by level.

cases.setup: const root = new TreeNode(3, new TreeNode(9), new TreeNode(20, new TreeNode(15), new TreeNode(7)));
const root = new TreeNode(1, new TreeNode(2, new TreeNode(4), null), new TreeNode(3));

note: Each node is enqueued and read once, so O(n) time; the widest level sets the extra space, up to O(n).
mapsTo: LC 102 · Binary Tree Level Order Traversal

concept: subtract the value on the way down
cue: At each node take away its value from the target; at a leaf check whether nothing is left over.
note: The path 5, 4, 11 reaches a leaf and adds to 20, so one root-to-leaf path matches the target.

cue: Subtract the node's value from the target and recurse; succeed only when a leaf's value equals what remains.
syntax: leaf test: left === null && right === null
prompt: Write `hasPathSum(root, target)` that returns `true` if some root-to-leaf path has node values adding up to `target`, else `false`. An empty tree returns `false`. A path must end at a leaf.

cases.setup: const root = new TreeNode(5, new TreeNode(4, new TreeNode(11, new TreeNode(7), new TreeNode(2)), null), new TreeNode(8, new TreeNode(13), new TreeNode(4, null, new TreeNode(1))));
const root = new TreeNode(1, new TreeNode(2), new TreeNode(3));
const root = new TreeNode(1, new TreeNode(2), new TreeNode(3));

note: The leaf test matters: stopping early at a node with one child would count a partial path that never reaches a leaf. O(n) time, O(h) space.
